title: Domain Policies and Rules
description: Define domain access control rules using five components, manage participants and permissions, and understand ACL resolution principles.

topics.title: Domain Access Control Rule Components

content.text: Five components define a domain access control rule. Each component answers a key question about when, where, and how access is controlled.
Five Components of an Access Control Rule

rows: Default domain for PTC New Publication Template product

content.text: A set of access control rules defines an access control list (ACL). The ACL is generated by evaluating and combining all applicable rules. You can view the ACL in the Policy Administrator by selecting an object type and life cycle state.
ACL Resolution Principles

The access control list is obtained by combining all rules that apply to an object's type, state, and domain. Rules from ancestor domains and ancestor types are included. For participants, grant and deny rules are merged separately.

cards.back: All rules from the domain and its ancestor domains are combined. All rules for the type and its ancestor types are combined.
All rules for a participant and their group/role membership are combined. Grant (+) rules are merged. Deny (-) rules are merged.
If no ACL entry exists, the participant's permission set is null (denied). If a participant is GRANTED by one ACL but DENIED by another, the permission is NOT granted.

content.prompt: Arrange the five components of an access control rule in their logical definition order:
items: Where - Define the domain and context
When - Specify the life cycle state

keyTakeaways: Five components define a rule: domain (where), object type (what), state (when), participant (who), and permission (how).
ACLs are generated by combining all applicable rules from the domain hierarchy and type hierarchy.
If a participant is both granted and denied the same permission, the permission is NOT granted.
Absolute Deny takes precedence over all other permissions including ad hoc rules.

topics.title: Participants and Permissions

content.text: When creating an access control rule, you select one principal (participant) and assign permissions. Understanding the different participant types and permission options is key to building effective rules.
Participant Types

rows: Rules apply to all members and subgroup members
Very strict - overrides group rules. Use sparingly.
Broad scope covering all organization members
Context team role, organization role, Pseudo-role All, Pseudo-role Owner
Owner can only be granted, never denied
Selected participant OR all except selected participant
Inverse deny restricts everyone except a specific group

content.text: User-specific rules should be used sparingly. They create very strict rules that are difficult to override. It is a best practice to manage access using groups and roles.
Permission Options
For each permission, you must select Grant, Deny, Absolute Deny, or None. When you grant certain permissions, Windchill auto-selects complementary permissions.

cards.back: Full Control (All), Read, Download, Modify, Modify Content, Modify Identity, Create, Set State, Revise, New View Version
Change Domain, Change Context, Change Permissions, Delete, Administrative. These control structural and administrative operations.
Selecting Grant for Create automatically grants Modify, Modify Content, Read, and Download. You can override this behavior by manually changing individual permissions.

content.prompt: Match each participant type to the correct description:

pairs.left: Can only be granted permissions, never denied
Restricts everyone except a specific group
Applies to all Windchill users in the system

keyTakeaways: Only one principal can be selected per rule - create multiple rules for multiple participants.
Granting to the Owner pseudo-role creates very strict rules superseding everything except absolute deny.
Windchill auto-selects complementary permissions when granting Create or other higher-level permissions.
The inverse option (all except participant) is powerful for restricting access to a specific group.

topics.title: Access Control Rule Strategies

content.text: When defining access control rules, the approach you choose significantly affects maintainability, security, and how future changes impact access. Three common strategies demonstrate the trade-offs.
Explicitly Granting and Denying Access

Rules applied to a type also apply to all its subtypes. Similarly, rules applied to a group also apply to all members and subgroups. A grant rule that is not explicitly set for any operation is considered a denial of that access.
Three Solution Approaches

rows: Grant broad access, then deny specific groups
Users in multiple groups may lose access; new subgroups inherit broad access
New subgroups still inherit broad access
Remove broad grants, explicitly grant each group
Most secure - access forbidden by default. Fewest rules, easiest to read
Requires adding a rule for every new group

content.text: The grant-only approach (Solution 3) is the most secure because access is forbidden by default. This additive approach simplifies reading and maintaining access control rules.
User Permissions Override Group Permissions

If a group is denied Read permission but a specific user in that group has a grant rule, the user can still access the object. This is because user-specific rules are stricter than group rules. To prevent this, use absolute deny at the group level.

content.prompt: Arrange these access control approaches from least secure to most secure:
items: Grant broad access, then deny specific groups (deny-based)
Use inverse deny to exclude non-authorized groups
Remove broad grants, explicitly grant each group (grant-only)
Use absolute deny for highly restricted content

keyTakeaways: Rules on a type apply to all subtypes; rules on a group apply to all members and subgroups.
The grant-only approach is most secure - access is denied by default, and each grant is explicit.
User permissions override group permissions, but absolute deny overrides everything.
Choose your strategy based on security requirements, maintainability, and organizational structure.

topics.title: Set a Product Context to Private

content.text: In this exercise, you set a product context to private to restrict access. Currently, any user from the PTC Publications organization can access released information. You will verify the impact of the change.
You will use two accounts: jgrisdam/ptc (a non-team member) and bpatterson/ptc (a business administrator).

content.title: Set a Product Context to Private
content.objective: Demonstrate how setting Private Access on a product prevents non-team members from searching for released objects.

steps.action: Launch a browser and click the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: This opens the Windchill home page.

steps.action: Click Sign In.
steps.detail: The sign-in page allows you to authenticate with your Windchill credentials.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill with username jgrisdam and password ptc.
steps.detail: The user jgrisdam is not a team member of the PTC New Publication Template product. They cannot see the product context. However, because the product is currently Public, they can search for released documents.

steps.action: Search Windchill for public*.
steps.detail: This searches for all objects with names starting with 'public' across all accessible contexts.

steps.action: From the Search Results table, browse to the Information page for PUBLICATION CHECK-LIST.
steps.detail: This opens the document's information page showing its attributes and state.

steps.action: Notice that the document is at a Released state.
steps.detail: Released objects are visible to users with Read access in public product contexts.

steps.action: Notice that the Context has the (Secured information) label.
steps.detail: User jgrisdam can see the document attributes. However, they cannot identify where it is stored.

tasks.title: Activate the Private Access for the Product

steps.action: Launch an incognito browser session or a different browser where you are not signed in as jgrisdam.
steps.detail: You need a separate session to sign in as the business administrator without logging out jgrisdam.

steps.action: Click the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: This opens the Windchill home page in the new session.

steps.action: Click Sign In.
steps.detail: The sign-in page allows you to authenticate as the business administrator.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill with username bpatterson and password ptc.
steps.detail: bpatterson is the business administrator who can change the product access settings.

steps.action: Browse to PTC New Publication Template > Details.
steps.detail: The Details page shows the product configuration including its access settings.

steps.action: Edit the product.
steps.detail: This opens the Edit Product dialog where you can change configuration settings.

steps.action: In the Edit Product dialog, set Private Access to Yes and click OK.
steps.detail: This moves the product's Default domain to be a child of the Private domain, bypassing global PDM rules.

steps.action: Browse to PTC New Publication Template > Team.
steps.detail: Notice that jgrisdam is not a member of the PTC New Publication Template team.

steps.action: Do not close the browser session.
steps.detail: You will return to this session to reactivate public access after verifying the restriction.

steps.action: Return to the browser session signed in as jgrisdam.
steps.detail: You need to verify that the private access setting has taken effect for non-team members.

steps.action: Search Windchill for public*.
steps.detail: Notice that the search results table is no longer showing the document. The private access setting has removed jgrisdam's ability to see released objects.

steps.action: Return to the browser session signed in as bpatterson.
steps.detail: You will now restore the product to public access.

steps.action: Browse to PTC New Publication Template > Details.
steps.detail: Navigate back to the product details to change the access setting.

steps.action: Edit the product.
steps.detail: This opens the Edit Product dialog.

steps.action: In the Edit Product dialog, set Private Access to No and click OK.
steps.detail: This reactivates the public access rules from the PDM domain, restoring visibility of released objects to all organization members.

steps.action: Close all browser sessions.
steps.detail: Setting a context as Private enables users to bypass the access control rule that grants Read permission to all Released objects set in the PDM domain. This configuration is inherited from the organization template General (PDM) used to create the organization.

keyTakeaways: Setting Private Access to Yes moves the product's domain under the Private domain, bypassing PDM rules.
Non-team members lose access to released objects when a product is set to private.
The Private domain has no out-of-the-box data access rules, enabling fully custom restrictive policies.
Private Access can be toggled without affecting the product's internal structure or team configuration.

topics.title: Set Domain Policies

content.text: In this exercise, you create access control rules in the Policy Administrator. You will grant technical writers the ability to create Outline documents and deny modifications during the review state.
You will use bpatterson/ptc (administrator) and kperez/ptc (technical writer) to create and test the rules.

content.title: Set Domain Policies
content.objective: Create domain access control rules to grant Technical Writers create access to Outline documents and deny modifications at the Under Review state.

steps.action: Launch a browser and click the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: This opens the Windchill home page.

steps.action: Click Sign In.
steps.detail: The sign-in page allows you to authenticate with your Windchill credentials.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill as a technical writer with username kperez and password ptc.
steps.detail: User kperez has the Technical Writer role in the PTC New Publication Template product.

steps.action: Browse to PTC New Publication Template > Folders.
steps.detail: The Folders page shows the folder structure of the product context.

steps.action: Click the New Document icon.
steps.detail: This opens the New Document dialog where you can select the document type to create.

steps.action: Confirm that Outline is not listed in the Type menu.
steps.detail: User kperez does not currently have create access to the Outline document type.

steps.action: Cancel the New Document dialog and close the browser session.
steps.detail: You have confirmed the current access limitation. You will now create a rule to grant this access.

steps.action: Launch a browser and click the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: This opens the Windchill home page for the administrator session.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill with username bpatterson and password ptc.
steps.detail: bpatterson is the business administrator with access to the Policy Administrator.

steps.action: Browse to PTC New Publication Template > Utilities.
steps.detail: The Utilities section contains administration tools.

steps.action: In the Business Administration section, click Policy Administration.
steps.detail: This opens the Policy Administrator for the PTC New Publication Template product context.

steps.action: Select the Default domain of the PTC New Publication Template context.
steps.detail: The Default domain contains the access control rules for data objects in this product.

steps.action: Collapse the Domains panel.
steps.detail: This gives more room for the rule table and access control list.

steps.action: In the Access Control Rules tab, click the View access control lists icon in the Search Result table.
steps.detail: This icon generates a summary report of all access control rules for a given object type or state.

steps.action: In the Type field, search for and select the Outline object type.
steps.detail: This filters the access control list to show rules affecting the Outline document type.

steps.action: From the State menu, select Creation, then click Search.
steps.detail: By default, the Technical Writer role has the same permissions as Team Members who have only Read and Download permissions at Creation state. User kperez cannot create an Outline document because Creation is the initial state for the document.

steps.action: Close the report.
steps.detail: You have reviewed the current access control rules and confirmed the gap.

tasks.title: Create a Rule at Creation State for Technical Writers

steps.action: From the Search Results toolbar, create a new access control rule.
steps.detail: This opens the New Access Control Rule dialog where you define the rule components.

steps.action: In the New Access Control Rule dialog, next to the Type field, click the Find icon.
steps.detail: This opens a type browser to search for and select the object type.

steps.action: Search for the Outline type in the tree, select the radio button, and click OK.
steps.detail: The Outline type is a subtype of WTDocument.

steps.action: From the State menu, select Creation.
steps.detail: This restricts the rule to only apply when Outline documents are in the Creation lifecycle state.

steps.action: In the Participant search field, type Tech, and select the Technical Writer (Context Team Role — PTC Publications) role.
steps.detail: This sets the participant for the rule to the Technical Writer context team role.

steps.action: In the Grant column, select Create and Set State permissions.
steps.detail: When you select the Create permission, Windchill automatically selects other complementary permissions that allow modifications (Modify, Modify Content, Read, Download).

steps.action: Notice that selecting Create automatically selects other permissions.
steps.detail: Windchill auto-selects Modify, Modify Content, Read, and Download when you grant Create, because it is normal to be able to modify and read content that you create.

steps.action: Click OK.
steps.detail: The new access control rule is created and added to the rule table for the Default domain.

tasks.title: Create a Rule to Deny Modifications for Under Review State

steps.action: From the Search Results toolbar, create a new access control rule.
steps.detail: You will create a second rule to deny modifications during the review process.

steps.action: In the New Access Control Rule dialog, search and select the Outline object type.
steps.detail: This sets the rule to apply to the Outline document type.

steps.action: From the State menu, select the Under Review state.
steps.detail: This restricts the rule to only apply during the Under Review lifecycle state.

steps.action: In the Participant search field, type PTC Pub and select the PTC Publications organization role.
steps.detail: This targets all users in the PTC Publications organization.

steps.action: In the Permissions table, in the Absolute Deny column, select Modify Content and Change Permissions.
steps.detail: Absolute deny ensures no user can override these restrictions with ad hoc or user-level grants.

steps.action: Click OK.
steps.detail: The deny rule is created. No user in the PTC Publications organization can modify Outline content or change permissions during the Under Review state.

tasks.title: Test the Outline Document Creation Rule

steps.action: Open an incognito browser session and sign in to Windchill as kperez/ptc.
steps.detail: You will verify that the Technical Writer role now has create access to Outline documents.

steps.action: Browse to PTC New Publication Template > Folders.
steps.detail: Navigate to the product folders where new documents can be created.

steps.action: Create a new document.
steps.detail: Click the New Document icon to open the New Document dialog.

steps.action: Notice that the Outline type is listed in the Type menu.
steps.detail: The Technical Writer role now has the necessary access to create Outline documents. The access control rule you created is working correctly.

steps.action: Cancel the New Document dialog and close the incognito browser session.
steps.detail: You have successfully verified the new access control rule.

keyTakeaways: Domain access control rules are created through the Policy Administrator utility.
Granting Create permission auto-selects Modify, Modify Content, Read, and Download.
Absolute Deny on Under Review state prevents any user from modifying content during review.
Always test rules by signing in as the affected user to verify the expected behavior.
